data.title: Paramount Gold Nevada Corp. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Paramount Gold Nevada Corp., a company focused on acquiring, exploring, and developing precious metals projects in the United States, has released its Form 10-K report for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the highly competitive mineral exploration industry.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Loss: $9.05 million. The company reported a net loss for the year ended June 30, 2025, which increased by approximately 12% compared to the previous year. This increase is attributed to higher exploration and development expenses, as well as increased professional fees and directors' compensation.
  • Loss per Common Share: $0.13. The loss per common share remained consistent with the previous year, reflecting the ongoing financial challenges faced by the company as it continues its exploration and development activities without generating revenue.

Business Highlights

  • Business Overview: Paramount Gold Nevada Corp. is engaged in acquiring, exploring, and developing precious metals projects in the United States, with a focus on Nevada and Oregon. The company aims to enhance project value through exploration and engineering programs to expand and upgrade mineral resources into reserves.
  • Key Projects: The company's material properties include the Sleeper Gold Project in Nevada and the Grassy Mountain Project in Oregon. These projects are in various stages of exploration and development, with ongoing efforts to complete technical studies and permitting processes.
  • Operational Highlights: During the fiscal year ended June 30, 2025, Paramount continued its permitting activities at the Grassy Mountain Project. The State of Oregon's Technical Review Team approved the Environmental Evaluation and all components of the mining, processing, and closure scenarios for the proposed operation.
  • Federal Program Inclusion: The Grassy Mountain project was selected for inclusion in the federal government's FAST-41 program, which provides comprehensive permitting timetables and collaborative management on the Federal Permitting Dashboard.
  • Reclamation Activities: Paramount is responsible for managing reclamation activities at the Sleeper Gold Mine, including ongoing monitoring, stabilizing, contouring, pond conversions, and re-vegetating activities as directed by the BLM and NDEP.
  • Human Capital Resources: As of June 30, 2025, the company employed four full-time employees and one consultant, with a focus on attracting and retaining talent through competitive compensation and benefits programs.
  • Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) Commitment: Paramount is committed to integrating sustainability into its business practices, focusing on minimizing environmental impact, engaging with local communities for economic development, and implementing strong governance practices.
  • Future Outlook: Paramount plans to continue its exploration and development activities, focusing on completing the permitting process for the Grassy Mountain Project and managing liquidity to support its operations. The company anticipates ongoing losses as it advances its projects towards production.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Focus: Paramount Gold Nevada Corp. is focused on advancing its Grassy Mountain Project through the completion of state and federal permitting processes. The company is also engaged in exploration and development activities at its Sleeper Gold Project. Strategic initiatives include managing exploration and engineering programs to expand and upgrade known mineral resources to mineral reserves, and participating in the federal FAST-41 program to streamline permitting processes.
  • Capital Management: The company has been actively managing its capital through an 'at the market' equity offering program, raising $2,356,709 in the fiscal year ended June 30, 2025. Paramount issued 4,728,165 shares under this program. Additionally, the company issued 3,845,273 shares for payment of interest on its outstanding Secured Royalty Convertible Debenture. The company has a Secured Royalty Convertible Debenture with Sprott for $15,000,000, which may be repaid in cash or converted into a royalty. The company is also managing its liquidity by maintaining a positive cash balance and working capital, and by reducing cash expenditures where possible.
  • Future Outlook: Paramount expects to continue incurring losses as it advances its exploration and development programs. The company plans to fund operations through existing cash, equity financings, and the sale of royalties. It anticipates spending $2.7 million on corporate, land claim maintenance, and general expenses, and $2.0 million on completing the permitting process at the Grassy Mountain Project. The company is exploring additional financing options to support its operations and maintain its properties, with a focus on securing construction financing for the Grassy Mountain Project.

Challenges and Risks

  • Competitive Industry: Paramount Gold Nevada Corp. operates in a highly competitive mineral exploration industry, facing competition from larger companies with greater financial resources.
  • Regulatory Challenges: The company is subject to extensive federal, state, and local regulations, which could lead to substantial costs and delays in exploration and development activities. The company’s operations are dependent on maintaining valid mining claims and obtaining necessary permits, which are subject to regulatory changes and challenges.
  • Uncertainty in Mineral Reserves: The estimation of mineral reserves and resources is imprecise and subject to significant uncertainty, which could adversely affect financial results.
  • Market Volatility: The volatility of precious metals markets directly impacts revenues and profitability.
  • Operational Risks: Mining operations are hazardous and pose environmental and insurance risks.
  • Going Concern: The company faces substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern due to significant losses and limited cash reserves. The company has not generated revenue from operations and requires significant additional capital for exploration and development.
  • Property Acquisition: The industry is highly competitive, and the company may struggle to acquire quality properties. Title to mineral properties can be uncertain, posing risks of ownership loss.
  • Exploration Costs: The company has no history of mining operations, and exploration costs may exceed expectations.
  • Price Fluctuations: Fluctuating gold and mineral prices, along with regulatory and environmental compliance, present ongoing challenges.
  • Foreign Currency Risk: The company is exposed to foreign currency exchange rate risk, primarily due to cash balances held in Canadian dollars. Interest rate fluctuations could impact the company’s cash balances, although the company does not use derivative instruments to manage these risks. The strengthening of the U.S. dollar could positively impact expenses transacted in Canadian dollars, while a weakening could increase costs.

SEC Filing: Paramount Gold Nevada Corp. [ PZG ] - 10-K - Sep. 25, 2025

data.title: Armlogi Holding Corp. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Armlogi Holding Corp., a key player in the warehousing and logistics industry, has released its annual 10-K report, providing a comprehensive overview of its financial and operational performance for the fiscal year. The report highlights significant growth in revenue, strategic expansions, and the challenges faced in a competitive and evolving market.

Financial Highlights

Revenue: $190.4 million, increased by $23.4 million or 14.0% from the previous year due to the addition of new warehouse locations and increased shipment volume.

Gross (loss) profit: $(3.0) million, a decrease from a gross profit of $18.1 million in the previous year, primarily due to increased lease expenses, temporary labor expenses for new warehouses, and UPS expenses.

(Loss) Income from operations: $(17.7) million, compared to an income of $8.1 million in the previous year, reflecting higher operating costs and expenses.

Net (loss) income: $(15.3) million, a decrease from a net income of $7.4 million in the previous year, driven by increased costs and a decline in gross profit margin.

Basic & diluted net (loss) earnings per share: $(0.37), compared to $0.19 in the previous year, reflecting the overall decline in net income.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company provides warehousing and logistics services, with revenue primarily generated from transportation services, warehousing services, and other services such as customs brokerage. Transportation services accounted for the largest portion of revenue, followed by warehousing services.

Geographical Performance: The company operates ten warehouses across the United States, strategically located near major ports and customer bases, including the West Coast, Midwest, and East Coast. A significant portion of revenue is generated from PRC-based customers, highlighting the company's strong presence in the Chinese market.

Sales Units: The company experienced an increase in shipment volume due to the addition of new warehouse locations, contributing to the growth in transportation services revenue.

New Product Launches: In January 2023, the company launched international ocean freight services, expanding its logistics offerings and enhancing supply chain efficiency for customers.

New Production Launches: The company expanded its operations by opening two new warehouses in the fiscal year ended June 30, 2025, including a new facility in Illinois, which required additional labor and resources.

Future Outlook: The company plans to expand its customer base in China and Southeast Asia, including Vietnam, Thailand, Indonesia, and the Philippines, as well as Mexico. It also aims to enhance supply chain efficiency by expanding the breadth and depth of its solutions and services, and further invest in supply chain technologies to drive sustainable growth.

Strategic Initiatives

Standby Equity Purchase Agreement (SEPA): The company has entered into a SEPA with YA II PN, Ltd., allowing it to sell up to $50 million of its common stock, providing flexibility in raising capital.

Capital Management: The company issued convertible notes with a principal amount of up to $21 million, subject to a 10% original issue discount, to support its capital needs. It also engaged in equity financing activities, issuing shares under the SEPA to raise $950,000. The company has been actively managing its lease obligations, with significant operating and finance lease liabilities for its facilities and equipment.

Future Outlook: The company anticipates that its current cash, along with cash generated from operating and financing activities, will be sufficient to meet its working capital requirements and capital expenditures for at least the next 12 months. It plans to continue leveraging the SEPA for additional capital as needed and will focus on optimizing its cost structure to improve profitability.

Challenges and Risks

Economic, Political, and Market Risks: The company faces significant competition in the U.S. warehousing and logistics market, which could lead to reduced margins and loss of market share. The ongoing U.S.-China trade conflicts and potential changes in trade policies pose risks to the company's revenue, as a substantial portion of its business is derived from PRC customers. Additionally, geopolitical tensions, such as the Russia-Ukraine conflict, could disrupt global markets and supply chains, adversely affecting the company's operations.

Operational Risks: The company relies heavily on third-party logistics providers, such as FedEx and UPS, for transportation services. Any disruptions in these services could negatively impact the company's ability to meet customer demands. Furthermore, the company is exposed to risks related to labor actions, such as port strikes, which could disrupt the U.S. transportation network and affect the company's business.

Regulatory Risks: The company is subject to numerous U.S. federal and state regulations applicable to the warehousing and logistics industry. Non-compliance with these regulations could result in penalties and disruptions to the company's operations. Additionally, changes in PRC government policies, such as restrictions on currency convertibility, could impact the company's ability to receive payments from PRC customers.

New and Emerging Risks: The company is facing new challenges due to inflationary pressures, which have led to increased costs for transportation, commodities, and labor. These pressures could impact the company's profitability if it is unable to pass on the increased costs to customers. Additionally, the potential for a recession could reduce consumer spending power in the U.S., affecting demand for the company's services.

Management Strategies: Management acknowledges the challenges posed by geopolitical tensions and trade conflicts, and is focused on diversifying its customer base and expanding its geographic footprint to mitigate these risks. The company plans to expand its warehousing network in key U.S. markets and explore opportunities in Southeast Asia and Mexico to reduce reliance on PRC customers.

Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks related to foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, which could impact its financial results. Additionally, the company faces risks associated with changes in interest rates and capital costs, which could affect its ability to finance operations and growth initiatives.

SEC Filing: Armlogi Holding Corp. [ BTOC ] - 10-K - Sep. 25, 2025

data.title: American Medical Association urge DHS to exempt physicians from new $100,000 H-1B visa fee
data.description: The American Medical Association and 53 leading medical societies has urged the U.S. Department of Homeland Security to exempt physicians from the newly announced $100,000 H-1B visa application fee, the groups said on Thursday.

data.title: Trump Has Taken Stakes in These Companies. How Their Stocks Have Fared. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Joe Light

President Donald Trump is exerting an unprecedented amount of control over private companies. So far, that's working out fine for most investors.

Companies that need something from the White House have found the help comes with strings attached.

Trump in June approved Nippon Steel's purchase of U.S. Steel, but only in exchange for veto power over some corporate actions. The Department of Defense bought 15% of rare earths miner MP Materials and a similar deal with Lithium Americas is in negotiations. The government took a 10% stake in Intel Corp. soon after Trump put pressure on its CEO to resign.

The moves are the biggest private sector power grab for the U.S. government outside of major economic crises. The results for shareholders are a mixed bag, but largely positive.

The biggest beneficiaries have been the rare earths miners, whose shares have doubled since news first broke of the government's potential involvement. Intel's shares have risen 24% since August's deal, compared with a 1.8% rise for the S&P 500.

Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices, which the White House said would give the U.S. 15% of their revenues from China chip sales, have seen their shares fall. The companies on recent earnings calls have said the deal isn't finalized, and China in September banned many of the chip sales in any case.

It's less clear how well the investments will work longer term, and the White House is explicit that it isn't losing sleep about how its actions impact private shareholders.

"It's not like we're trying to set up a hedge fund or something to get an internal rate of return on our Intel investment," a White House official said. "We want to make sure that if we're intervening to help an American company that we're doing so in a way that taxpayers can reap the upside."

In mid September, the White House used its control over Nippon Steel to nix a company decision to stop processing steel at a U.S. Steel plant in Illinois. After reversing course on the plant closure, U.S. Steel said it had "found a solution" to continue production.

"For Nippon Steel idling that plant was perhaps a sensible business decision, but for us in the United States...that represents a key loss to our domestic capacity," the White House official said.

Write to Joe Light at joe.light@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: TechnipFMC Gets Contract for Offshore Project With ExxonMobil Guyana
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

TechnipFMC has been awarded a contract with ExxonMobil Guyana Limited to supply subsea production systems for its Hammerhead project in Guyana.

The company, which provides technology for the energy industry, said Thursday that it defines a "substantial" contract as between $250 million and $500 million.

Under the contract, TechnipFMC will provide project management, engineering and manufacturing of subsea production systems to support production and water injection capabilities.

The contract marks TechnipFMC's seventh greenfield project award from ExxonMobil Guyana since 2017, the company said.

The Hammerhead project is expected to begin production in 2029. It will have the capacity to produce about 150,000 barrels of oil per day, ExxonMobil said Monday.

ExxonMobil Guyana is an affiliate of ExxonMobil.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Trump Signs Executive Order to Keep TikTok Alive — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Trump signed an executive order on Thursday that is designed to pave the way for a TikTok deal between the U.S. and China.

The executive order is another step in the Trump administration's efforts to keep TikTok running in the U.S., after Congress sought to ban the social media app in 2024. The deal cannot be considered complete until China signs off on it, industry experts said.

"We wanted to keep TikTok operating, but we also wanted to make sure that we protected Americans' data privacy as required by law, both because it's the right thing to do, but also because it's a legal requirement of the law that was passed last year by Congress," Vice President JD Vance said at the executive order signing on Thursday. "So we think that we were able to do that. Of course, we're going to keep on working at it,"

White House officials laid out the framework for the deal earlier this week.

Before the singing of the executive order on Thursday, Liu Pengyu, a spokesman for the Chinese embassy, reiterated earlier comments about TikTok, noting the government would be happy to see "productive commercial negotiations in keeping with market rules lead to a solution that complies with China's laws and regulations and takes into account the interests of both sides" and said the U.S. needs to provide an "open, fair and non-discriminatory environment for Chinese investors."

Former President Joe Biden signed the Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act into law in April 2024 that banned TikTok and other apps owned by ByteDance, unless those apps get sold to an owner that isn't controlled by a foreign adversary.

The law was intended to address worries that a Chinese-owned algorithm could threaten U.S. national security by controlling the content fed to U.S. users.

But that algorithm is also an important piece of the TikTok puzzle, since its recommendations make the app popular and sticky with users. Details on how the new entity will control the algorithm remain unclear, but Vance said on Thursday that the deal ensures the American entity and the American investors will control the algorithm.

"We don't want this used as a propaganda tool by any foreign government, " Vance said.

China would have to sign off on the algorithm changes, according to Gavekal director of tech research Laila Khawaja.

"It still requires review and approval from Beijing because the recommendation algorithms are on China's Catalogue of Technologies Prohibited or Restricted from Export as an export-restricted technology, " Khawaja said. "This is similar to U.S. export control regime where Beijing will need to review and issue an export permit."

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1613 ET - Reddit has a pipeline of new revenue opportunities at its disposal as it looks to close its monetization gap, Baird analysts say. The company's search business has a roughly $400 billion dollar domestic market opportunity, with a text-oriented app expected to contribute monetization next year, they say. Shopping ads are also a key medium-term opportunity as Reddit launches performance ads with measurable return on investment in the coming year, they say. The platform is also gaining traction internationally through machine translation, they add. Those opportunities, among others, continue to provide upside potential, they say, raising their price target on the stock to $240 from $177. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1421 ET - Netflix is beefing up its ad business, most notably with a recent partnership with Amazon Ads that should support improved advertising onboarding, flexible buying, better performance and higher ad spend, say JPMorgan analysts. Netflix is also rolling out interactive ads and its Netflix Ads Suite should drive faster execution and improve measurement and targeting. The analysts say Netflix's Ad Tier is tracking ahead of management's expectations year-to-date and is on pace to roughly double advertising revenue in 2025. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1337 ET - Netflix's original content and diversification can mitigate competitive impacts from media industry consolidation, say JPMorgan analysts. Paramount Skydance preparing a bid for Warner Bros. Discovery has sparked concern among Netflix investors that a larger Paramount would increase competition and hurt its ability to license third-party content. But 62% of Netflix's content assets are originals and no title receives more than 1% of total viewing, limiting the impact of consolidation. And while it's unlikely, Netflix could also participate in the consolidation in the long-term by acquiring a leading studio, giving it "access to a significant library of high quality content," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1135 ET - The EU's Digital Markets Act is good for European consumers and should be protected, European consumer advocacy group BEUC says after Apple called for the bloc's executive arm to repeal the law. Apple said Wednesday that the rulebook--designed to curb the market power of the world's largest tech groups--is making it harder to bring new features to the region. "It is disappointing that Apple is continuing to resist change and trying to delay compliance every step of the way," Agustin Reyna, BEUC's director general, says. "Apple is misrepresenting what the DMA stands for: more competition and fairer digital markets which both consumers and businesses, especially SMEs, need," he says. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

1025 ET - As mortgage rates drift into the low 6% range, a new Realtor.com report found that metros with younger, more mobile populations, such as Washington D.C., Denver, Colo., Raleigh N.C. and Virginia Beach, Va., stand to benefit the most from consistently lower mortgage rates. Realtor.com says 81% of existing mortgages have a rate of 6% or lower. In other words, as mortgage rates approach the 6% level, you can expect to see more homeowners "unlocked," especially in areas with high mortgage usage. Miami, Buffalo and Pittsburgh rank among the least mortgage-reliant metros, suggesting their housing markets may be slower to respond to falling rates. For buyers, particularly first-time purchasers, easing mortgage rates can unlock affordability and expand choices. For sellers, the outlook depends more on geography. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0919 ET - Oracle's $18 billion six-tranche bond deal on Wednesday, increased from an initial $15 billion on the back of exceptionally strong demand, "is the latest sign that the AI investment boom, long the focus of equity markets, is now spilling into credit," says TwentyFour Asset Management's Johnathan Owen in a note. The blockbuster issuance follows Oracle's landmark $300 billion, five-year cloud infrastructure contract with OpenAI, alongside speculation of further partnerships with Meta and its potential hosting of TikTok's U.S. algorithm, the portfolio manager says. "Oracle's $18 billion raise highlights both the scale of investment required for the buildout of AI and cloud infrastructure, and the market's demonstrable willingness to finance it on competitive terms," Owen says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0645 ET - Data-center operators including VNET Group could benefit from Alibaba's long-term AI spending and IT power plans, Nomura analysts Ethan Zhang and Bing Duan say in a note. After a muted 2Q, orders are projected to rise in 2H, following a new contract in for its internet data center in Hebei province, and potential renegotiations in Jiangsu province. VNET's plan to raise capital expenditure and capacity delivery capability in 2H 2026 could encourage the company to bid for more projects. Nomura maintains a buy rating with a $13.20 target on VNET's ADRs, which last closed at $10.37 (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0503 ET - Baidu's artificial-intelligence chips are likely still inferior to Nvidia's H20 and need to be combined to maximize computing efficiency, Morningstar analyst Kai Wang says in a research note. Markets have cheered Baidu's AI chip development over the past month and sent its shares up almost 50% so far this month. However, the analyst thinks that Baidu's chips are likely one to two generations behind Nvidia's H20 chips, as Kunlun P800 is a 7-nanometer chip while Nvidia's H20 is a 4-nanometer chip. Although Baidu could be "self-sufficient and incur lower capital expenditure without having to buy Nvidia H20s, its [chip's] performance and measure as a long-term solution remains unproven," the analyst adds. Baidu's shares ended 2.3% higher at HK$131.60. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0449 ET - Kingsoft Cloud's HK$2.8 billion share sale, announced Tuesday, is expected to ease capital pressure for meeting AI computing demand from its customers and the ecosystems of Xiaomi and Kingsoft, Nomura analysts say. The 338 million-share sale, its second offering in six months, represents stronger-than-expected demand for AI infrastructure from LLM training and inference markets, analysts Bing Duan and Ethan Zhang say in a note. Kingsoft Cloud is seen as benefitting from the rising demand more than peers, as it allocates most of its computing power to clients, unlike larger rivals balancing internal usage. Nomura maintains a buy rating and a HK$9.20 target price on the stock, which last closed at HK$16.47.(jason.chau@wsj.com)

0445 ET - China's Quora-like platform Zhihu could benefit from AI, according to Citi analysts in a research note. Zhihu will upgrade its AI search function for professional content, expand its knowledge database and introduce new functions by the end of this year to better integrate AI with the user community, Citi notes, citing management's remarks. Zhihu could unlock content value in the AI era with more monetization potential. Citi notes its attractive valuation, with net cash higher than market cap by 42%. Citi keeps a buy rating with a target price of US$7.0 for its ADRs, which last traded at US$5.22. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0330 ET - STV Group's absence of interim dividend isn't surprising, Panmure Liberum analysts Johnathan Barrett and Sean Kealy write in a note. The Scottish broadcaster didn't declare an interim dividend for the first-half due to the challenging environment against last year's interim dividend of 3.9 pence a share. The recent slowdown has placed significant pressure on its two divisions, they say. A market recovery would support improved profitability and potentially allow for the restart of dividends, they add. Shares are down 2.2% at 112 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0146 ET - Kuaishou Technology's artificial-intelligence video generator Kling AI and other AI enablers could drive a re-rating in the Chinese tech company's shares, says DBS Group Research analysts in a note. They put Kling AI's addressable market at around US$60 billion in a bull-case scenario, citing the platform's improving model consistency. Kuaishou's generative AI-powered OneRec model could also offer upside in advertising and e-commerce through better recommendations. They project Kuaishou's 2025, 2026 and 2027 adjusted earnings to grow by 16%, 20% and 18%, respectively. DBS reinstates coverage on Kuaishou with a buy rating and a HK$98.00 target, citing its loyal and undermonetized user base. Shares gain 1.3% to HK$77.85. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

SEC Filing: KELLY SERVICES INC [ KELYA ] - Form 4 - Sep. 25, 2025

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1151 ET - Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem says economic policy from the country's governments have leaned too heavily on fiscal-stimulus measures designed to increase consumer demand. "We've overrelied on priming demand and not spent enough time on the tougher decisions of structural reform and making the investments that we need to actually grow the economy over the long term," says Macklem, according to remarks in an interview with Bloomberg News. His comments somewhat echo a speech he delivered this week, in which he warned Canada could either remain static and suffer income losses from US trade policy, or implement structural reforms and champion the building on new trade corridors to help set the stage for future growth. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1001 ET - Pending U.S. home sales dipped roughly 1% from a year earlier during the four weeks ending Sept. 21, the first decline in nearly three months, according to Redfin. The weekly average mortgage rate has fallen to 6.26%, the lowest level in nearly a year and down from roughly 6.9% at the start of summer. The dip was in anticipation of the Fed's first interest-rate cut of the year. But steadily falling mortgage rates haven't brought many homebuyers to the market. And while mortgage applications to refinance homes jumped 58% week over week during the second week of September, mortgage-purchase applications rose just 3%. Homebuying demand isn't yet improving because of stubbornly high home prices, still-high mortgage rates, and lack of new homes to choose from.(chris.wack@wsj.com)

0213 ET - Despite solid gains for MSCI's China Index since U.S. Liberation Day tariffs, it could rise further as the country's AI development gains momentum, Capital Economics says. "China is particularly well-positioned to benefit if enthusiasm for AI continues," notes Chief Markets Economist John Higgins. He notes the big-tech sector's weighting at around 60% of the MSCI China Index, higher than in its U.S. index. Noting that MSCI China's underperformance against the MSCI USA Index in recent years was largely due to faster U.S. EPS growth, Higgins says this EPS gap could narrow if China allows firms to benefit from plans to integrate AI across the economy. "And even if that didn't happen, the relative valuation of China's stock market remains lower than before the pandemic," he says. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

2130 ET - Forsyth Barr is happy with general insurer Tower's reset to its reinsurance costs. Analyst James Lindsay says Tower's renewal of its reinsurance program for FY 2026 was achieved on better-than-expected terms. Reinsurance costs are likely to fall from 13.3% of gross written premium in FY 2025 to 10.7% in FY 2026, Forsyth Barr says. Meanwhile, the maximum cover for the first two events has increased to NZ$915 million from NZ$800 million. "The program simplifies protection for large individual property risks, with Tower opting to retain increased underwriting risk in return for higher premiums," Forsyth Barr says. "We see this as a sensible trade-off in light of Tower's risk-based pricing capability, and view the placement of the program positively overall." It keeps an outperform call on Tower. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2124 ET - Srisawad Corp. looks on track for stronger earnings growth due to some tailwinds such as loan growth and net-interest-margin expansion, Thanachart Securities' Rawisara Suwanumphai says in a report. The financial services provider's loans could grow 3% in 2025, 8% in 2026 and 8% in 2027, partly driven by title loans, the analyst estimates. The Thai company's net interest margin should expand to 15.2% in 2026 and 15.4% in 2027 from 14.7% in 2025 thanks to growth in higher-yield title loans and declining funding costs. The brokerage forecasts Srisawad's earnings to grow 2% in 2H, 14% in 2026 and 13% in 2027. It raises its target price on the stock to THB36.00 from THB25.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares last closed at THB30.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1900 ET - The sale of Deterra Royalties' gold offtake contracts returns its focus to industrial metals, differentiating the company from gold-royalty peers, Macquarie analysts say in a note. "Those looking to have exposure to the industrial metals complex for lower beta may find DRR an attractive alternative to higher operationally leveraged exposures," say the analysts. They reiterate a "neutral" rating and A$4.20 target. Deterra last traded at A$4.19. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1827 ET [Dow Jones]--Regal Partners's share price continues to trade well below all-time highs, despite positive operating metrics that include an 8% rise in funds under management over July-August. Bell Potter believes this may relate to the conversion of various classes of stock into ordinary shares. "There are currently 356 million ordinary shares on issue, with 13 million shares due to convert at end-September, and a further 62 million shares converting in the next few years," says analyst Marcus Barnard. Bell Potter raises its price target by 15% to A$4.10/share, and says this assumes full conversion of the outstanding stock. Regal Partners ended Wednesday at A$2.92. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1519 ET - Crude futures recover early losses and end the session mixed with continued concerns over Russian supplies keeping sellers cautious. "There's no doubt that right now that the Russian hits on their oil infrastructure by Ukraine means that that really is Ground Zero," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. With Europe planning tougher sanctions on Russian oil and gas, it "could create a situation where we could start to see significant change in market psychology and a potential breakout to the upside." WTI slips 1 cent to $64.98 a barrel, and Brent settles up 0.2% at $69.42. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1350 ET - Oil futures are pulling up from earlier lows with continued support from tensions over the Russia-Ukraine war. Yesterday's WTI break above the 200-day moving average was "a technical signal that suggests a bullish market trend," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. WTI briefly retested the 200-day moving average as support, "and successfully held the level." he adds. WTI is off 0.1% at $64.91 a barrel and Brent is near flat at $69.29. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0943 ET - Activity in Canada's natural resources sector weakened sharply in the last quarter. Real GDP in the sector contracted 2.4% in 2Q, a reversal after growth of 1.8% in the first three months, Statistics Canada data shows. That was the largest decrease since 2Q 2020 and significantly deeper than the 0.4% decline in economy-wide GDP for the quarter. The declines for the sector were broad, with a notable 4.9% fall for forestry, 2.5% drop for energy and 1.2% decline for minerals and mining. The quarter was marked by an overall 4% fall in natural resources prices, including a steep decline for crude oil, and a 6.6% slump in sector export volumes even as imports increased 6.6%. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0910 ET - Oil futures pull back after two sessions of gains with increasing tensions over the Russia-Ukraine war keeping prices up despite concerns about imminent oversupply. Russia risk "makes shorting many parts of the complex very tricky indeed," Neil Crosby of Sparta Commodities says in a note. While the market looks better supplied post-summer and macroeconomic risk is rising, Russia and wider geopolitical risk is also higher, he says. "No wonder flat price has been so range-bound this summer." Of the opposing upside and downside risks, "at the moment it does look like the Ukraine situation is winning out, but the mood may change by next week." WTI is down 0.5% at $64.68 a barrel, and Brent is off 0.4% at $69.04.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0218 ET - TotalEnergies' buyback cut signals tougher market conditions that should drive it to reduce near-term investment, RBC analysts write. The French energy group late Wednesday said it planned a fourth-quarter share buyback to $1.5 billion, down from its recent quarterly rate of $2 billion. The company is likely to announce a reduction in organic capital expenditure for 2026 to $16 billion-$17 billion from about $18 billion previously presented at its Sept. 29 annual strategy update, RBC says. The analysts expect TotalEnergies to announce an option of further trimming spending as a protective strategy during future downturns. Shares rose 2.1% Wednesday to close at 53.50 euros and little changed year-to-date. (william.gray@wsj.com)

2246 ET - Pertamina Geothermal Energy is likely a prime proxy for bets on geothermal energy, Bahana Sekuritas' Jeremy Mikael says in a research report. The Indonesian renewable energy company offers baseload electricity generation, making it more reliable compared to other renewable sources, the analyst says. Also, the company's valuation is more reasonable than that of other listed geothermal peers in Indonesia, reinforcing a scarcity value. The Indonesian company is on track to add 1.1GW over next decade with planned capacity additions totaling 280MW for 2027-2028, while its total capital expenditure for 2025-2034 is expected to reach $4.3 billon. The brokerage initiates coverage of the stock with a buy rating and a target price of IDR1,700.00. Shares are 0.7% lower at IDR1,370.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2118 ET - Infratil's CDC data-centers business just took a big step toward derisking a target of doubling Ebitda across FY 2025-2027, Forsyth Barr says. CDC has signed an additional 100MW of contracts. This means it now has 95% of FY 2027 revenue contracted. "We increase our FY 2027 Ebitda estimate 7% due to this higher contracting, and are now in line with its A$660 million target," analyst Ben Crozier says. The contracts were signed just in time, according to Forsyth Barr. To achieve the Ebitda target, CDC needs most of its FY 2027 contracts to start generating revenue early in the year. Infratil's FY 2027 period starts in six months, and Forsyth Barr says the time from contract signing to revenue generating appears to sit at around 3-6 months. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1859 ET [Dow Jones]--Amplitude Energy's A$150 million capital raising looks sensible to Bell Potter. That's because Amplitude's capex commitments will rise after management expanded a drilling program to support its East Coast Supply Project and signaled the potential restart of the Patricia Baleen field in southeastern Australia. Bell Potter says drilling a fourth well, specifically to target the Nestor deposit, could take ECSP capex to A$485 million-A$580 million. "We see the Nestor option further de-risking the ECSP through a portfolio effect," analyst Stuart Howe says. Bell Potter retains a buy call on Amplitude, and trims its price target by 3.4% to A$0.28/Share. Amplitude ended Wednesday at A$0.235. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1427 ET - Gold futures settle slightly higher on strong physical metal flow. Gold ETFs have seen inflows totaling $10.5 billion in September, says Citi Research in a note. Year-to-date, the flow to ETFs is roughly $50 billion. "ETF has outshined all other gold demand sectors this year and is the single most important contributor to the gold price rally in our view," says Kenny Hu of Citi Research. Front-month gold futures close up 0.1% to $3,736.90 a troy ounce, for the third-highest finish on record. Today marks the fourth positive session in the past five for gold. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1346 ET - Worthington Steel's hot-rolled coil pricing is pressured by softer volumes in most of its end markets, resulting in wider holding losses, Chief Financial Officer Tim Adams tells analysts. Current pricing is about $800 per ton, down from a peak of $950 in March, he says. The steel processor expects to generate inventory holding losses in its F2Q of $5 million to $10 million, compared with the $5.6 million in the current quarter, Adams says. The lower prices come as tariffs on imported steel increased in June. Worthington Steel slides 7% to $31.54. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1316 ET - Worthington Steel's automotive market is finally making a comeback, Chief Financial Officer Tim Adams says during an analyst call. The sector was one of the few bright spots during F1Q, as shipments increased 17% from the prior year. The auto market itself is improving and the company now expects to finish the year with a 15 million unit build rate, up from prior forecasts of 13.5 million. Worthington also anticipates tailwinds from potential interest rate cuts. Beyond improving industry trends, Worthington has also won market share by ramping up volumes and new programs, executives say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1301 ET - Freeport-McMoRan's stock offers an attractive buying opportunity as share falls on lowered production forecasts after a mudslide at one of its mines, Jefferies analysts say in a note. While the mudslide is a clear negative for the company, some of this downside was already reflected in the share price because the market had been expecting a downgrade to guidance, the analysts say. The disruption should also lead to a tighter copper market and a higher copper price, which would be positive for the company's operations in the Americas. "We would buy FCX on today's weakness," they say. The stock falls 5%, to $35.78, extending its week-to-date decline to 21%.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1252 ET - Worthington Steel is facing soft demand due to macroeconomic uncertainty, Chief Financial Officer Tim Adams tells analysts. Agriculture is the most challenged market, as volumes were down 50% year-over-year due to continued softness in the agricultural equipment sector, Adams says. Shipments to the heavy truck market fell 7% and to construction customers fell 3%. Toll processing volumes fell 22% because of less processing from mills and service centers, as well as the closure of a processing plant in Ohio, Adams says. "We experienced pluses and minuses across various markets as customers continued to navigate uncertainty during the quarter," he said. Shares fall 6.4%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1239 ET - Freeport-McMoRan 2027 production levels could also be at risk after the company cut its guidance for this year and next year after a fatal mudslide at one of its mines, Jefferies analysts say in a note. Management now expects negligible sales volumes at the mine in the fourth quarter and is guiding to as much as 35% lower volumes in 2026 compared to its prior forecast, the analysts say. "There is downside risk to 2027 production as well, in our view," they say, noting the Grasberg mine is one of the largest and lowest cost mines in the world, accounting for about 3% of global mined copper supply. They lower their price target on the stock to $46 from $48. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1234 ET - Freeport-McMoRan's forecast for a hit to production from a recent mudslide at its flagship Indonesia copper-gold mine is worse than expected, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. The company's initial view for next year points to production at the mine that could be about 35% below prior guidance, marking the second major setback for the mine after a smelter fire last year, the analysts say. The analysts establish a December 2026 price target on the stock of $46, from their prior December 2025 target of $56.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1200 ET - Freeport-McMoRan's cuts to its Grasberg copper production guidance for this year and next year increases the magnitude of expected deficits in the global copper market, supporting upside for copper prices, UBS analysts say in a note. Copper demand has remained resilient in China, and while demand from traditional end-use sectors in the U.S. and Europe remains weak, demand from grids and renewables remains robust, the analysts say. They estimate refined copper demand growth of about 3% next year versus supply growth of less than 1%, which they expect to "push the market into deficit resulting in drawdowns in visible inventory that will support sustainable copper price upside." (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

0943 ET - Activity in Canada's natural resources sector weakened sharply in the last quarter. Real GDP in the sector contracted 2.4% in 2Q, a reversal after growth of 1.8% in the first three months, Statistics Canada data shows. That was the largest decrease since 2Q 2020 and significantly deeper than the 0.4% decline in economy-wide GDP for the quarter. The declines for the sector were broad, with a notable 4.9% fall for forestry, 2.5% drop for energy and 1.2% decline for minerals and mining. The quarter was marked by an overall 4% fall in natural resources prices, including a steep decline for crude oil, and a 6.6% slump in sector export volumes even as imports increased 6.6%. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0904 ET - Gold futures turn negative after weekly jobless claims fall to 218,000 from 232,000, while economists surveyed by WSJ forecast the layoff indicator at 235,000. The surprise decrease eases concerns about the job market, and looks to curb the appetite of safe haven investors. The U.S. dollar index also turns positive following the release. Most-active gold futures are down 0.1%, to roughly $3,763 a troy ounce. Silver has pared some early gains too, but remains up 1.3% to $44.79 a troy ounce. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0612 ET - Palm oil closed higher after data signaled improvement in exports in September. Malaysia's palm oil exports for Sept. 1-25 likely rose 11.31% from a month earlier to 1,185,422 metric tons, cargo surveyor AmSpec Agri Malaysia said. Crude palm oil futures "ended higher on the back of stronger exports and expectation of weak production in coming weeks," says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. He sees support at 4,350 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,580 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia derivatives contract for December delivery closed 61 ringgit higher at 4440 ringgit a ton. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0356 ET - Shares in London-listed miners rose in early trading after copper prices surged overnight on Freeport McMoRan's declaration of force majeure at a crucial mine. Copper futures on the London Metal Exchange climbed to their highest levels in over a year after Freeport declared force majeure on supplies contracted from its Grasberg mine in Indonesia. The U.S. mining group's statement renewed supply concerns as the Grasberg site constitutes 3.2% of global copper supply and 70% of Freeport's copper production. Rio Tinto and Glencore shares rose 2.1% and 0.9% respectively, while Anglo American shares were up 1%. (william.gray@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1505 ET - Electric vehicle sales in the U.S. are expected to hit a record of around 409,000 in the third quarter, as consumers take advantage of government-backed incentives slated to end Sept. 30, Cox Automotive said. The prior record was set in the fourth quarter of 2024, with 365,824 units sold, according to Cox. The share of EV sales in the third quarter will likely also be a record, coming in at close to 10% of total sales, Cox added. "The federal tax credit was a key catalyst for EV adoption, and its expiration marks a pivotal moment," said Stephanie Valdez Streaty, director of Industry Insights at Cox Automotive. "This shift will test whether the electric vehicle market is mature enough to thrive on its own fundamentals or still needs support to expand further." Sales of EVs are expected to slow in the fourth quarter, although long-term sales growth will continue, Cox said. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1456 ET - Cox Automotive said the September new-vehicle sale pace is expected to be around 16.2 million, up from 15.8 million in September 2024 and higher than August's 16.1 million. Year over year, third-quarter sales volume is expected to rise 6.2%, reaching 4.14 million. "The new-vehicle sales pace has been surprisingly strong this summer and through the third quarter as uncertainty around tariff policy has decreased," said Charlie Chesbrough, senior economist at Cox Automotive. "Continued low inflation and unemployment rates, coupled with a strong stock market, have kept consumers in a buying mood," Chesbrough added. Sales numbers have been helped by an increase in EV sales, as buyers move to make purchases before tax credits expire at the end of September, Chesbrough said. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1316 ET - Worthington Steel's automotive market is finally making a comeback, Chief Financial Officer Tim Adams says during an analyst call. The sector was one of the few bright spots during F1Q, as shipments increased 17% from the prior year. The auto market itself is improving and the company now expects to finish the year with a 15 million unit build rate, up from prior forecasts of 13.5 million. Worthington also anticipates tailwinds from potential interest rate cuts. Beyond improving industry trends, Worthington has also won market share by ramping up volumes and new programs, executives say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1147 ET - CarMax says its loan loss provisions for 2Q rose to $142.2 million from $112.6 million the previous year, citing worsening performance among 2022 and 2023 vintage loans. CEO Bill Nash says on the analyst call that in 2022 and 2023, consumers were flush with cash from the pandemic and hadn't yet fully experienced inflation. They then started to struggle more, leading CarMax to adjust its extension policy. Nash says that with those extensions "we wanted to see how much was going to come back in delinquency. And unfortunately, during the quarter, we saw more revert back to delinquency and loss."(nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1139 ET - CarMax is seeing a pullback in used car buying from high-credit score customers and better sales among customers with lower credit scores. "Your mid to high FICO customers, they seem to be sitting on the sidelines a little bit," CEO Bill Nash says on the analyst call. "The one area that's maybe not down is probably low FICO 550 and below." The company says that division by credit score was becoming clear throughout the credit industry. "We can talk to other lenders, other dealers. You can see it in the credit bureaus. It's apparent," says Jon Daniels, executive vice president of CarMax Auto Finance. CarMax slides 20% after sales and profits plunged in the latest quarter. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0409 ET - The German automotive industry continues to face significant challenges, despite the U.S. retroactively reducing tariffs on imports of passenger cars and car parts from the EU, the president of the German association of the automotive industry, Hildegard Mueller, says. Although the reduction is an important step, the burden imposed by additional tariffs on a range of steel and aluminum products remain, she says in a comment. The EU must continue to work to improve the framework conditions for transatlantic trade, Mueller says. The agreed cooperation on regulatory matters is an opportunity for both sides of the Atlantic and should form the basis for a positive agenda, she adds. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0350 ET - European defense stocks fall across the board in early morning trade Thursday, after surging on the back of the comments made by President Trump on the Ukraine war on Tuesday evening. Babcock International Group loses 2.5%, Chemring falls 1.8%, Hensoldt is down 2.2%, Rheinmetall drops 1.4% and Leonardo decreases 1.3%. Safran and Thales fall 0.7% and recover from deeper drops in opening trade. On Wednesday, European defense stocks led the Stoxx Europe 600 list of risers, after Trump said Kyiv could win back "all of Ukraine" and that Russia looks like a "paper tiger." (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0210 ET - The Philippines' aviation sector is likely the central pillar to connect some growth drivers, such as airline expansion and a recovering tourism industry, Maybank Securities' Ronalyn Joyce Lalimo says in a research report. Capital is being invested to expand the Ninoy Aquino International Airport, the New Manila International Airport and regional hubs, as well as 10 public-private partnership projects worth PHP181 billion, the analyst notes. Supporting measures include the country's PHP7.5-billion Aviation Transport Infrastructure Program and 15-bundled airport upgrades worth PHP8 billion. The brokerage's top sector pick is Cebu Air, which has a buy rating and a target price of PHP48.00. Shares are 0.6% lower at PHP32.55. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2335 ET - SATS is unlikely to be largely affected by softer U.S. air-cargo demand after the removal of the de minimis exception, says CGS International analysts in a note. Without the exception, which allowed parcels below US$800 to enter the country tax-free, U.S. air-cargo demand may weaken. Still, the analysts say any drag on global demand will likely be limited as e-commerce companies pivot to other markets. They expect SATS' cargo-business growth to outpace the industry with its cross-regional operations. They retain their conservative 2026-2028 EPS estimates given the air-cargo handler's sensitivity to macroeconomic conditions, but still expect an attractive 15.4% net profit CAGR over the same period. CGS International reiterates an add rating and a S$3.83 target on SATS, which falls 0.9% to S$3.42. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

2306 ET - NIO's stock has been volatile following the release of a key sports utility vehicle over the weekend. The automaker's shares listed in Hong Kong gained leading up to the launch but dropped sharply earlier this week before recovering some of the losses. The fall was likely driven by the view that NIO has no more near-term catalysts after the launch, says Eugene Hsiao, head of China equity strategy at Macquarie Capital. Product launches by other Chinese EV makers such as XPeng and Li Auto led to similar stock movements in the past, he says. Shares are up 2.1% at HK$56.60. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

data.title: China shipyard orders strong despite US port fees on China vessels, report says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Chinese shipyards capture 53% of Jan-Aug global ship orders by tonnage
  • US port fees on China-linked vessels aim to counter China's maritime dominance
  • MSC orders 12 containerships from China despite US port fees

By Lisa Baertlein

Global shipping companies are moving full steam ahead with commercial vessel orders from Chinese shipyards, despite the U.S. targeting those ships with port fees aimed at countering China's maritime dominance, a new report from the Center for Strategic and International Studies showed.

Chinese shipyards captured 53% of all global ship orders by tonnage during the first eight months of 2025, according to the CSIS analysis of S&P Global data released on Wednesday.

That was on par with full-year 2023 levels before the U.S. Trade Representative (USTR) launched the China maritime probe that paved the way for the port fees, CSIS said.

"Shipping companies are largely moving forward with business as usual," said Brian Hart, a fellow with the China Power Project at CSIS and an author of the report. "So far, it doesn't look like these policies will achieve a significant shift away from China."

China's share of global ship orders by tonnage had jumped to 73% in 2024, suggesting shipowners were seeking to lock in contracts before potential USTR restrictions took effect.

Starting October 14, ships built in China - or operated or owned by Chinese entities - will need to pay a fee at their first port of call in the United States.

That fee could top $1 million for a ship carrying more than 10,000 containers and is slated to rise annually through 2028, according to analyst estimates.

The port fees on China-linked vessels are part of a broader U.S. effort to revive domestic shipbuilding and to blunt China's growing naval and commercial shipping power.

But catching up with China's state-supported shipyards is a heavy lift.

Last year, the U.S. shipyards built fewer than 10 commercial ships, while China's turned out well over 1,000, military and industry analysts said.

China over the last two decades has propelled itself to the No. 1 position globally and its biggest shipyards handle both commercial and military projects.

Meanwhile, the U.S. Navy's fiscal year 2025 plan said U.S. commercial shipbuilding has experienced a near-total collapse and called for the long-term revitalization of that industry to bolster Navy shipbuilding.

MSC - the largest containership operator - placed orders for 12 of those vessels to be built in China since USTR announced the port fees in April this year, the CSIS report said.

Switzerland-based MSC, like peers Hapag-Lloyd , Maersk and CMA CGM, has taken China-linked ships off U.S. trade routes, limiting or negating the new fees.

HSBC analysts said China's COSCO Shipping is most exposed with estimated 2026 port fees of $1.5 billion.

President Donald Trump has been a cheerleader for U.S. shipbuilders, seeking alliances and investments from powerhouses like South Korea.

data.title: Tesla Stock Falls. Its European Sales Still Stink. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Tesla stock edged lower early Thursday after data showing its sales results in Europe were still struggling.

Shares of the electric vehicle maker were down 0.4% at $440.90 in premarket trading, while S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures were down 0.1% and up 0.1%, respectively.

The move followed the European Automobile Manufacturers' Association's (ACEA) release of sales data for August. Tesla sold 8,220 vehicles in the European Union, down 37% year over year. BYD sold 9,130 cars, up 201% year over year. It was the second consecutive month that ACEA data showed BYD with the leg up.

Including the U.K., Norway, and other countries in the European Free Trade Association (EFTA), Tesla surpassed BYD, selling 14,831 cars compared with BYD's 11,455 vehicles. Tesla sales dipped 22% year over year. BYD sales grew 216%.

Tesla has sold 133,857 cars in the EFTA so far this year, down 33% year over year. BYD sold 95,940, up 280%.

Tesla has struggled to grow car sales in 2025, amid more competition, an aging product lineup, and fears that CEO Elon Musk's position in the Trump administration turned off some core Tesla buyers — politically left-leaning people looking to go green. Globally, Musk's EV maker sold 721,000 cars in the first half of the year, down about 13%. For the third quarter, Wall Street expects sales of about 447,000 vehicles, down about 3% compared with the 463,000 sold in the third quarter of 2024.

Tesla shares gained 4% on Wednesday after Musk defended his proposed pay package, awarding him some 425 million incentive-laden stock options worth about $1 trillion if he hits all his performance goals, including generating $400 billion in annual earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, or Ebitda.

For comparison, Apple — another member of the so-called Magnificent Seven group of megacap stocks — is expected to generate about $145 billion in 2025 Ebitda, according to FactSet. Tesla is expected to generate about $13 billion.

The annual meeting isn't until November. Investors have a while to decide how they will vote. Given their history of approving his 2018 options award twice, few expect the package to be voted down.

Coming into Thursday trading, Tesla stock was up about 10% so far this year and up about 74% over the past 12 months. It has been a wild few weeks for the stock. Shares were up 27% over the past 10 trading sessions.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: CSX Stock Has Had a Bumpy Ride. It's Time to Buy, Says Wells Fargo. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

After a rocky few weeks, shares of CSX look like a buy to Wells Fargo, with several catalysts on the horizon that could lead to faster earnings growth.

Thursday, Wells Fargo analyst Christian Wetherbee upgraded shares of CSX to Buy from Hold. His price target went to $40 from $37.

"Recent operational headwinds are clearing, and new commercial agreements (and customer diversification efforts) will likely drive volume outperformance," wrote Wetherbee.

In August, Berkshire Hathaway's railroad BNSF and CSX announced a collaboration for intermodal shipping. Intermodal refers to freight that travels by both railroad and truck for portions of the shipping journey. That could mean up to $375 million more annual revenue for CSX, Wetherbee wrote. Another catalyst cited was the end of two construction projects, which will reduce operating spending.

CSX stock was up 0.6% at $3 in early trading, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average were down 0.6% and 0.3%, respectively.

Coming into Thursday trading, CSX stock was up about 5% this year, but down about 5% since Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern announced merger plans in July. The combination could mean stiffer competition for CSX if it passes regulatory muster.

BNSF and CSX, for their parts, don't seem too interested in trying to combine, creating another transcontinental railroad to oppose Union Pacific and Norfolk. "While BNSF owners have not expressed interest in a merger with CSX, we continue to believe the competitive environment may necessitate further consolidation if the Union Pacific/Norfolk Southern merger is approved," added Wetherbee. The potential for a merger "likely leads to a sustained elevated multiple for CSX."

He raised his 2026 earnings per share estimates to $1.90 from $1.80, valuing shares at 21 times his estimate. CSX stock has traded for closer to 16 times estimated next year's earnings over the past few years.

Overall, 69% of analysts covering CSX stock rate shares Buy, according to FactSet. The average Buy-rating ratio for stocks in the S&P 500 is about 55%. The average analyst price target for CSX stock is about $38.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Lithium Miner Stock Jumps Again. What a U.S. Stake Could Look Like. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Anita Hamilton

The prospect of the U.S. taking a stake in Canadian miner Lithium Americas has everyone excited, even though no one's quite sure how it might work.

After doubling Wednesday, the company's stock rose another 23% Thursday to $7.37, lifting Lithium Americas' market value to more than $1.5 billion.

As to the specifics of how a renegotiation of the Energy Department's $2.3 billion loan might work, analysts at National Bank of Canada Financial Markets recently laid out their bets on the most likely scenarios — and which would be best for investors.

In the most likely scenario, Lithium Americas would issue warrants to the government representing 5% to 10% of either the company or its sole project at Thacker Pass in Nevada. Reuters previously reported that the company has already offered the warrants to the government at no cost. In return, Lithium Americas could get more time to pay back the loan, improving its cash flow. The warrant would give the government the right to buy stock at a future date at a preset price.

The second most likely option would involve a purchase guarantee on the 40,000 metric tons a year of battery-grade lithium carbonate that the refinery is expected to produce starting in 2028. That could work in a couple different ways. Lithium Americas' partner General Motors, which owns around a third of the Thacker Pass project, could expand its commitment to buy the chemical compound that it currently plans to use in its Ultium battery cells powering its electric vehicles.

Alternately, there could be a set price floor to ensure sufficient revenue flow to keep the mine and processing plant afloat. That could involve the U.S. making up the difference between the actual sales price of the lithium carbonate produced and the price needed to ensure profitability. A floor price is important because lithium prices have fallen around 65% over the past two years, although they are expected to rise again as demand increases.

The least likely option, in the analysts' assessment, is a premium equity offering. While it would provide an additional influx of funds and help to derisk the project, it would also dilute shareholder value. That would also be the least favorable outcome for investors.

Based on recent Trump administration deals with other companies such as Intel and rare-earth miner MP Materials, it's also possible that a new agreement could involve some combination of all of the above, along with other stipulations such as the government having a say in how the project is run.

Write to Anita Hamilton at anita.hamilton@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Here is one often-overlooked reason why U.S. stocks have been steadily climbing over the past 15 years
data.description:
By Joseph Adinolfi

It is a simple matter of supply and demand, according to JPMorgan's Michael Cembalest

Investment strategists can cite a number of reasons why stocks have marched steadily higher since the financial crisis. But at the end of the day, supply and demand dynamics might be playing a bigger role than many realize.

Investment strategists can rattle off a number of reasons for why stocks have marched steadily higher since the 2008 financial crisis.

For the biggest U.S. companies, profit margins have steadily expanded and earnings have become less volatile, according to a team from Bank of America Global Research. Floating-rate debt has been nearly eliminated from the balance sheets of companies in the S&P 500 SPX. The rise of American Big Tech firms like Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) and Apple Inc. (AAPL) has had a transformative impact on the global economy, all while generating enormous profits.

But at the end of the day, an imbalance between supply and demand might be playing a bigger role than many realize.

Or at least so said Michael Cembalest, the chairman of the markets and investment strategy group at JP Morgan Asset Management. In a report published earlier this week, Cembalest described this as one of the most misunderstood factors helping to shield stocks from volatility.

"I'm not a market technician, but I do believe that declining net equity supply conditions since 2011 have contributed to U.S. equity-market resilience in the face of various shocks. If you believe that supply and demand conditions affect the price of goods and labor, there's every reason to believe such conditions can affect financial-asset prices as well," Cembalest said in the report.

In the chart below, Cembalest showed how the supply of U.S. stocks in dollar-value terms has generally been shrinking since 2011. The COVID-19 pandemic market boom, which saw a flurry of IPO activity, was a brief, if notable, exception.

Corporations buying back their own shares have contributed to this trend, Cembalest said.

Meanwhile, contributions from defined benefit and defined contribution payment plans have ensured a steady bid for stocks. And while these contributions have declined over the years as a percentage of the total value of the S&P 500, $1.5 trillion in annual inflows still need to wind up somewhere, he added.

To be sure, investors have grappled with a number of stock-market selloffs since 2011. But only two have transformed into genuine bear markets: The bear market of 2022, which led to the worst calendar-year performance for the S&P 500 since 2008, and the COVID-19 selloff in March 2020. The latter was short-lived, with stocks recovering all of their losses by the end of the summer of 2020.

U.S. stocks finished lower on Thursday, with the S&P 500, Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA and Nasdaq Composite COMP all declining. It was the first time since March that all three indexes have tallied three-day losing streaks.

-Joseph Adinolfi

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Scilex Holding to Invest $150M of Bitcoin in Datavault AI
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Scilex Holding will make a $150 million bitcoin investment in Datavault AI.

The company, which acquires and commercializes non-opioid pain management products, said Thursday the investment aims to help Datavault accelerate its supercomputing infrastructure, expand independent data exchanges, and unlock new revenue streams.

Under the agreement, Scilex will receive up to about 278.9 million shares of Datavault common stock at a price of 54 cents per share, with 15 million shares issued at the closing of the initial tranche of Scilex's investment. The remainder will be issued in a second tranche.

Datavault's board and management remain unchanged, though Scilex will have the right to nominate two directors to its board, so long as Scilex maintains beneficial ownership of at least 10% of the company's common stock.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: BlackBerry Ltd reports results for the quarter ended August 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• BlackBerry Ltd reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 4 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended August 31, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported breakeven earnings per share. The mean expectation of five analysts for the quarter was for earnings of one cent per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 1 cents to 2 cents per share.

  • Revenue fell 10.6% to $129.60 million from a year ago; analysts expected $122.06 million.

  • BlackBerry Ltd's reported EPS for the quarter was 2 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $13.3 million.

  • BlackBerry Ltd shares had fallen by 5.3% this quarter and gained 8.6% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts was unchanged in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is 1 "strong buy" or "buy," 4 "hold" and 1 "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the software peer group is "buy."
